title: 第 47 節
en: v] ABLAUT. 34I and a, before y, while in light roots the vowel series is always a-a-a and never i. A knowledge of these limits has proved a useful check on many etymologies which formerly seemed unassailable, while a knowledge of what is legitimately possible in vowel changes has placed other derivations on a much safer basis. The only point on which I differ from Prof. Hiibschmann is the order of the stages through which, according to him, vowels have to pass as they are either weakened or strengthened. I think we should always start from the Level-stage (Grundstufe), which is either reduced on one side to the Low-stage (Tiefstufe), and the Loss-stage (Schwundstufe), or raised on the other to the Highstage (Hochstufe), and the Long-stage (Dehnstufe). In many cases therefore I should call what others call Low-stage, Level-stage, as, for instance, Sk. budh, vid, while I see in Sk. bodh and ved a Highstage, and in Sk. baudh and vaid a Long-stage. This, however, does not affect the facts, but only the principle. A large number of facts in illustration of these Ablaut changes may be seen in Hubschmann's and Brugmann's works. A few instances must suffice for our present purpose. The six series of vowel change according to Brugmann (i, p. 248) are : —
(i) e series : 0, e, o, e, 6.
(ii) e series : 0, 33 ', e, 6.
(iii) a series : 0, ae, a, 6.
(iv) 6 series : 0, 33, 6.
(v) a series : 0, a(o), a, 6.
(vi) o series : 0, o, 6.
These are the postulated Proto- Aryan representatives, which are varied according to the vowel 1 This as or a appears generally as 1.

system peculiar to each of the Aryan languages. Sanskrit, for instance, does not distinguish in writing between e, o, a, and it represents long e, a and 6 by a. It has, on the other hand, the advantage of a separate sign for the sonant ri and li. In Latin the vowels are written differently in different periods of the language. In Gothic e and o are missing, and represented at once by I and u, though differing probably in pronunciation.
Instances.

(i) Instances of the e series : — Thus in the e series, which is most largely represented, we have in Sanskrit the level form ped (written pad) weakened to pd and bd in upabdi (noise), raised to pad in the high form pad, nom. sing, pad, foot, gen. padas. In Greek we have the level form in 7re£d?, the high form in iroha, the long form in ttovs and 7rws. In Latin we have the corresponding stages in pedis, tripodare, and pes. In the same series we find the level form of the termination tri in pitW, as in pitn'shu, the high form in pitarau, the long form pita(r). In Greek we have the level form narepa, lowered in iraTpa<ri and Trarpos, raised in iraTrip and in compos, irarcop. In Latin we have only patris and pater ; in Gothic we should have fadrs, fadrum, and fader (O.N). I take e'x^, Sk. sah, as the level stage, weakened in €-a-)(ov, strengthened in 0^09. Likewise pat, weakened in pap-tur, strengthened in patayati ; kar, weakened in krttas and Aakre, strengthened in akaram and £akara. Budh is strengthened in bodhati and bauddha, and never weakened. Vid is strengthened in veda and vaidya, and never v] INSTANCES. 343 weakened. In Gothic, the level form which we find in stig-um, is raised to steig-a, lengthened in staig ; as bud-um is raised to biuda and bau]?, but never weakened. The root bend appears on its level-stage in binda (*bandha), on its high-stage in band (Sk. babandha), on its low-stage in bundum (for budum). In Greek we have fxivoi, [xovrj and jxl^-poj, reuco, tovos, and toltos ; e\nrov, XetVw and XeXoina ; e(j)vyov and (fievyco. In Latin perfldus, fido and foedus, &c.
(ii) Instances of the e series : — Dadhami, tlOt)^, Gothic gadeths, weakened in dadhmas, Tid{e)^ev, in hitas, 06to<;, strengthened in flood's, in Gothic doms.
(iii) Instances of the a series : — Dor. tcrrajott, ecrrav, tish^hami, astham, weakened in sthita, o-rard?, status, tasthur.
(iv) Instances of the 6 series : — Dadami, dxlam, StSw/xt, eSojv, donum, weakened in adita, Soros, datus, in dadmas, 818(0)^, dattas ; strengthening impossible.

(v) Instances of the a series : — Agkmi, ayw. ago, weakened in a^as, ay 6s, in #man, oyfxos (?), strengthened in %is, o-rpaT-qyos, ambages. Yd#ami, a^o/xaL, weakened in ish^a, i(/ur, strengthened in iyaz/a, ayakshit.
(vi) Instances of the o series : — Root od, in o£ei, odor, oleo, strengthened in oSwSe. Stem go (gau), /3oe?, bovis, strengthened in gaus, fiovs, bos, weakened in sugus.

Whoever recollects Bopp's review of Grimm's German Grammar, will be surprised at the progress that has been made in a systematic treatment of these vowel changes in the principal Aryan languages. But he will nevertheless admire Bopp's sagacity, even when we know now that he was on 344 INSTANCES. [CHAP.

a wrong road, and he will probably be startled at the extraordinary mastery of facts displayed by the ancient Sanskrit grammarians who in the end became the best guides of such scholars as Holtzmann, Benfey and others in helping them to unravel the confused web of the ancient accents, and using that thread in their bold exploration of the labyrinth of the Aryan vowel changes. It is easy now to sneer at Bopp's work, and to say ' that a broad and heavy mantle of charity needs to be drawn over it.' Those who like myself have lived through all the stages of these controversies, who have in turn been followers of Grimm, of Bopp, of Holtzmann, Benfey, De Saussure, Brugmann, and Hlibschmann, know how difficult it often was to advance from one point of view to another, nay, how long they had to labour before they mastered the simplest elements of the Sanskrit accent, as explained in Boehtlingk's Versuch in 1843, and in my Sanskrit Grammar (1886), and how difficult it often was to shake off as no longer tenable what for many years they had held as absolute truth. It is sometimes quite as difficult to give up what for many years one has held to be true as to learn a new truth or a new terminology. It is all the more pleasant to read a work like Bechtel's excellent Indogermanische Lautlehre, 1892, where a sketch of the labours of successive scholars is given, so far as the treatment of vowels and consonants is concerned, and where, though we see how much of the work of Grimm, Bopp, Schleicher and others has now become antiquated, never a word is uttered of disparagement ; on the contrary, credit is given for all honest work, whether its results are at present accepted or rejected.
v] ASSIMILATION, J. SCHMIDT. 345 Assimilation, J. Schmidt.

Nor is there likely to be peace or rather rest, not to say stagnation, for some time to come. The march of the army of comparative philologists is going on as brisk as ever, and some of the latest discoveries of Hiibschmann and Brugmann have by this time been left far behind by new discoveries. It was seen that the rules of the Ablaut, of the weakening and strengthening of vowels, however minutely worked out by our best scholars, were broken when we least expected it (Noreen, Abriss, $ 11). As no exception could well be tolerated, reasons had to be discovered for these exceptions, and one of these reasons, viz. assimilation of vowels, has of late been very fully treated by Professor J. Schmidt, in Kuhn's Zeitschrift, xxxii, p. 321 seq. We must remember, first of all, that the representation of the three vowels comprehended under the Sk. a, namely the e, o, a in Greek, and their corresponding sounds in the other Aryan languages, has never been reduced as yet to anything like law and order. Why we say (fidpofxev but ^epere, why we say e/3So^o5 and septimus for Sk. saptama, we cannot tell. In Sanskrit we have the practical rule that before m and v the final a of the verbal stem is lengthened. But why we have to say bharami, bharamas and bharavas, but bharasi and bharati, even the old Sanskrit grammarians cannot tell us. It is easy to say that it is due to the influence of the consonants m and v, but what is the nature of that influence ? One thing only is clear, whatever the cause may have been, its effects had begun to appear before Greek and Sanskrit were com- 346 ASSIMILATION, J. SCHMIDT. [chap.
pletely separated, for the Greek o is here the regular representative of the Sk. a, particularly in words where a represents the VWddhi stage.

Sometimes it would seem that e and o were used simply for the sake of differentiating closely allied roots. Thus I have little doubt that the root of o£w, 68, was originally, as pointed out by Benfey, the same as that of ia$io) (Or. und Occid., vol. i, p. 626), i.e. e'S. Taste and smell are very nearly allied. A thing that eats well, tastes well, smacks well (schmeckt gut). For the sake of differentiating the two meanings, the vowel of the root was in very distant times differentiated, giving us for eating ddmi in Sanskrit, ISojugu, c'ScuSt; in Greek, itan, at, etum in Gothic ; for smelling, in Greek, o£w, oSajSa, in Latin, odefacio and olefacio, odor, &c. Instead of c'ScuStJ we should, according to Schmidt's rule, expect oScoStj, but in order to keep the derivatives of the two varieties of the root apart, ihoohtj may have been allowed to keep its e. But this is not all. There are many words, particularly in Greek, where a, e, o interchange, sometimes in classical Greek, sometimes in the Greek dialects. Here, too, certain rules prevail, or, to speak more cautiously, here too some observations have been made which to a certain extent account for these changes.

Thus, as Professor Schmidt has lately shown l, (i) e should be assimilated to a following o, if unaccented, and if followed by X, p, ju, F, XX, 8, and k. Hence we find 6fioX6<; by the side of 6/3eXo's as in 6/3eXio-Kos, cudXos (originally 0,10X05) by the side of dteXo? in aieXovpos, and aiXovpos. We find e/3So/xo9 1 Kuhn's Zeitschrift, xxxii, p. 321.

v] ASSIMILATION, J. SCHMIDT. 347 for ejSSe/AOS as in e^e^rfKovra, ifiSefxcuov, &c. There is 7rrd\e/ao? and rpt7TToXojao5, TTToXepaios and tttoXojucuog. 'AtreXXojv should therefore be looked upon as more original than 'AttoXXcov, '^pyopevos than Op^ofxevog, AeX<f>oi than Ao\<j>oC, just as <£peVe9 is more original than <f)poves in Trpotfipoves, and the Aeolic eSoi/Te?, teeth, more original than oSovres, the eaters.
(ii) Schmidt's second observation refers to words in which e becomes o before originally accented v, such as KepKvpa changed to KopKvpa.
(iii) E, if unaccented, is assimilated by following a ; e.g. 'E/caS^'/xeia becomes 'A/caSi^eia, SeXapLVLOs SaAa/xi^aos, Kecra^Spa Kacra^opa.
(iv) A is assimilated to e, if e is unaccented ; e. g. apery becomes iperij, drep and arap become irepos, dyxeAvo? becomes iyx^Xvos.
(v) Unaccented a is assimilated to following o ; e. g. afxepyoj and ojxopyvvjxL ; crab's and crowds ; a/xa and 6//.09.

(vi) A (before or after />, a) is assimilated to o, particularly if followed directly or indirectly by v and Fo, provided the a was originally a heightened e ; for instance, 6p06s from *Fap9F6s, 6 py via, opvvpi, crTopvvfjLi, oWvfiL. In all these cases we expect dp and dX, for Sk. ri, just as for Vn'tra, we expected 'ApOpos, but find "OpOpos. That the restriction of being followed directly or indirectly by v or Fo, is not always observed, is shown by the retention of the o of opvvpi in opaeo, oparj, by 'OpOla (not urdhva) and by "OpOpos instead of "Apdpos = Sk. VWtra.
(vii) Initial unaccented o is assimilated to a ; e. g. oorrafcds, crab, and do-ra/cd? ; dcrrpdyaAos became do-TpdyaXos ; oSayfxos and dSay/xds (cf. 68d£).
zh: 以及在 y 之前的 a，而在輕根中元音級系始終是 a-a-a，絕不是 i。對這些限制的認識已經證明對許多曾經看似無可置疑的詞源學論證是一種有用的制約，同時對元音變化中何者屬於合法可能性的認識已經使其他推導建立在更加穩妥的基礎之上。我與胡布施曼（Hubschmann）教授的唯一分歧之處在於，根據他的觀點，元音在被弱化或強化時所必須經歷的階段的順序。我認為我們應當始終從級別階級（Grundstufe）出發，它既可以在一方面被縮減至低級階級（Tiefstufe）和消失階級（Schwundstufe），也可以在另一方面被提升至高級階級（Hochstufe）和延長階級（Dehnstufe）。因此在許多情況中，我應當把其他人所謂的低級階級稱為級別階級，如梵文的 budh、vid，而我在梵文的 bodh 和 ved 中看到的是高級階級，在梵文的 baudh 和 vaid 中看到的是延長階級。然而這不影響事實本身，只涉及原則問題。說明這些元音階級變化的大量事實可見於胡布施曼和布魯格曼的著作中。為了本書的目的，幾個例證應已足夠。布魯格曼所列舉的六個元音變化級系（i, p. 248）如下：——
（i）e 系列：0、e、o、e、6。
我檢視了您提供的"英文原文"，發現這不是連貫的英文文本，而是看似數學符號或數據序列： (ii) e series : 0, 33 ', e, 6. 這段內容並非穆勒《宗教學導論》中的散文或學術論述，無法進行有意義的翻譯。 可能的情況： - 這是文本掃描或轉錄的殘片 - 符號在傳輸過程中損毀 - 這是圖表或註記的標記 敬請提供完整且清晰的英文原文段落，我將提供準確的繁體中文翻譯。
（三）一個級數：0、ae、a、ō。
（五）一個序列：0, a(o), a, 6.
（vi）o 級數：0, o, 6.
這些是假定的原始雅利安代表，根據元音而有所變化。¹ 此「as」或「a」通常顯現為「1」。 ¹ 譯注：此處原文可能有排版或抄錄之誤。
各雅利安語言所特有的系統。例如梵文在書寫中不區分 e、o、a，而是用 a 代表長 e、a 和 ô。另一方面，它具有為濁音 ri 和 li 設立獨立符號的優點。在拉丁文中，母音在語言的不同時期書寫方式不同。在哥德語中，e 和 o 缺失，並同時由 i 和 u 代表，儘管發音上可能有所不同。
例證

在最廣泛出現的 e 系列中，我們在梵文中有平級形式 ped（拼寫為 pad），在 upabdi（噪音）中弱化為 pd 和 bd，在高級形式 pad 中升高，單數主格 pad（足），屬格 padas。在希臘文中，我們有平級形式 πεδ-, 高級形式 πόδα, 長級形式 πούς 和 πώς。在拉丁文中，我們有 pedis、tripodare 和 pes 中相應的階段。在同一系列中，我們找到詞尾 tri 的平級形式，如在 pitṛ̥ 中，見於 pitṛ̥shu，高級形式在 pitarau，長級形式 pitá(r)。在希臘文中，我們有平級形式 πατέρα，在 πατράσι 和 πατρός 中降低，在 πατήρ 和複合詞 πατέρ 中升高。在拉丁文中我們僅有 patris 和 pater；在哥德語中我們應該有 fadrs、fadrum 和 fader（O.N.）。我將 ἔχω、梵文 sah 視為平級階段，在 ἐ-α-χον 中弱化，在 ὄχος 中強化。同樣地，pat 在 pap-tur 中弱化，在 patayati 中強化；kar 在 kṛ́ttas 和 ἄκρε 中弱化，在 ákaram 和 ἄκαρα 中強化。Budh 在 bodhati 和 bauddha 中強化，從不弱化。Vid 在 veda 和 vaidya 中強化，從不弱化。在哥德語中，我們在 stig-um 中發現的平級形式升高到 steig-a，在 staig 中延長；正如 bud-um 升高到 biuda 和 bau-, 但從不弱化。根 bend 在其平級階段出現於 binda （*bandha），在其高級階段出現於 band（梵文 babandha），在其低級階段出現於 bundum（對應 budum）。在希臘文中我們有 μῖνοι、μόνη 和 μίγ-ηρος、λεύκο、τόνος 和 τολτός；ἔλιπον、λείπω 和 λέλοιπα；ἐφυγον 和 φεύγω。在拉丁文中 perfidus、fido 和 foedus，等等。
（二）e 系列的實例：——Dadhami，古哥特語 gadeths，在 dadhmas 中減弱，Tid(e)^ev，在 hitas 中，ὄστος，在 flood's 中強化，在古哥特語 doms 中。
（三）a 級數的例示：—— 多利安方言 terragon、terrān、tishṭhāmi、astham，在 sthita、o-rard?、status、tasthur 中有所減弱。
（iv）第六級數的例證：——Dadami、dxlam、StSw/xt、eSojv、donum，在 adita、Soros、datus、dadmas、818(0)^、dattas 中呈現減弱；強化不可能。
（五）a 系列的實例：— Agkmi、αγω、ago，在 a^as、ay ός 中弱化，在 #man、oyχος（？）中，在 %is、o-rpaT-qyos、ambages 中強化。Yd#ami、a^o/xaL，在 ish^a、i(/ur 中弱化，在 iyaz/a、ayakshit 中強化。
（vi）ο 系列的例證：——詞根 od，見於 o£ei、odor、oleo，其強化形式為 oSwSe。詞幹 go（gau），βoe?、bovis，其強化形式為 gaus、βovs、bos，其弱化形式為 sugus。
凡是還記得波普對格林《德語語法》評論的人，都會對在主要雅利安語言中對這些元音變化進行系統性處理所取得的進展感到驚訝。但即使我們現在已知波普當時的觀察還不夠全面，人們仍將欽佩他的敏銳洞察力。344 個實例。[第

一條錯誤的道路，他將可能會為古代梵文法家所展現出來的非凡事實掌握力感到驚訝。這些古代梵文法家最終成為了霍爾茨曼（Holtzmann）、本費（Benfey）及其他學者的最佳嚮導，幫助他們理清古代重音的混亂網絡，並用那條線索在雅利安元音變化的迷宮中進行大膽探索。現在嘲笑博普（Bopp）的著作，並說「需要用寬厚慈悲的厚重外衣來掩蓋它」是很容易的。像我這樣經歷過這些爭論的所有階段的人，曾先後是格林（Grimm）、博普、霍爾茨曼、本費、德‧索緒爾（De Saussure）、布魯格曼（Brugmann）及赫利布施曼（Hlibschmann）的追隨者，他們知道從一個觀點推進到另一個觀點往往是多麼困難，甚至在掌握梵文重音的最簡單要素之前要勞碌多久——正如博特林克（Boehtlingk）在1843年的《嘗試》（*Versuch*）以及我在1886年的《梵文法》（*Sanskrit Grammar*）中所解釋的那樣——以及放棄那些多年來被奉為絕對真理的觀點是多麼困難。放棄多年來被認為是真實的東西，有時與學習新真理或新術語一樣困難。因此讀到貝希特爾（Bechtel）1892年的優秀著作《印歐語言聲學》（*Indogermanische Lautlehre*）就更加令人愉快了，該著作在元音及輔音的處理方面提供了歷代學者勞動成果的概要，並且儘管我們看到格林、博普、施萊歇爾（Schleicher）及其他人的多少工作現已過時，卻從未說出任何貶低的言辭；相反地，對所有誠摯的工作都給予讚譽，無論其成果目前是被接受還是被拒絕。
同化，J‧施密特。345 同化，J‧施密特。

因此，在相當長的一段時間內，不太可能出現和平，或者說是安寧，更不用說停滯了。比較語文學家軍隊的行進一如既往地迅速進行，而希布施曼（Hiibschmann）和布魯格曼（Brugmann）最近的一些發現，此時已經被新的發現遠遠拋在身後。人們發現，元音弱化與強化的「消融律」（Ablaut）規則，無論我們最優秀的學者如何細緻地闡發，卻在我們始料未及之處遭到打破（諾林《語法綱要》第11節）。由於幾乎不能容許任何例外存在，人們必須為這些例外尋找原因，其中一個原因，即元音同化現象（assimilation of vowels），最近得到了施密特教授（Professor J. Schmidt）在庫恩《時刊》（Kuhn's Zeitschrift）第32卷第321頁及以後的充分論述。首先，我們必須記住，包含在梵文 a 之下的三個元音，即希臘文中的 e、o、a，以及它們在其他雅利安語言中的相應音素，至今還未曾被化約為任何類似法則與秩序的東西。我們為何說 φέροιμεν 而不是 φέρε，為何說 ἔβδομος 而對梵文 saptama 說 septimus，我們無法解釋。在梵文中，我們有一條實用規則：在 m 和 v 之前，動詞詞幹末尾的 a 要加長。但為何我們必須說 bharami、bharamas 和 bharavas，而不是 bharasi 和 bharati，連古梵文語法家都無法告訴我們。很容易說這是由於輔音 m 和 v 的影響，但那種影響的性質是什麼呢？只有一件事是清楚的：無論其原因為何，它的效果已經在希臘文和梵文分化之前就開始出現了。
完全分開，因為希臘文的 o 在此乃梵文 a 的規則對應，特別是在 a 代表吠檀多（Vddhi）階段的詞彙中。

有時似乎 e 與 o 純粹是為了區分親緣甚近的詞根而被使用。因此我毫不懷疑 o£w 的詞根，如班費(Benfey)所指出的(Or. und Occid., vol. i, p. 626)，原本與 ia$io)的詞根相同，即 e'S。味覺與嗅覺的關係極為密切。一樣美味可口的東西，嘗起來好(schmeckt gut)。為了區分這兩種含義，詞根的元音在極其遠古的時代便被區分開來，在梵文中給出了進食義的 ddmi、ISojugu、c'ScuSt；在希臘文中，itan、at、etum 在哥德文中；在嗅覺方面，在希臘文中是 o£w、oSajSa，在拉丁文中是 odefacio 與 olefacio、odor 等。與其說 c'ScuStJ，按照施密特規則，我們應該期待 oScoStJ，但為了保持詞根這兩種變體的衍生詞相互區別，ihoohtj 或許被允許保留其 e。但這還不是全部。在許多詞彙中，特別是在希臘文中，a、e、o 相互交替，有時在古典希臘文中出現，有時在希臘方言中出現。這裡同樣也存在著某些規則，或者說得更謹慎些，這裡同樣也有一些觀察成果在一定程度上說明了這些變化。

因此，如施密特教授最近所證明的¹，(i) 若 e 後接無重音的 o，且其後跟隨 X、p、ju、F、XX、8 及 k，則 e 應同化於後續的 o。因此我們發現 6fioX6<；與 6/3eXo's 並存，如在 6/3eXio-Kos、cudXos（原為 0,10X05）與 aieXovpos 及 aiXovpos 中的 dteXo? 並存。我們發現 e/3So/xo9¹ ¹ Kuhn's Zeitschrift, xxxii, p. 321.

同化現象，施密特著。347 頁 ejSSe/AOS 如同 e^e^rfKovra、ifiSefxcuov 等情形。存有 7rrd\e/ao? 和 rpt7TToXojao5、TTToXepaios 和 tttoXojucuog。因此，'AtreXXojv 應視為比 'AttoXXcov 更為原始，'^pyopevos 比 Op^ofxevog 更為原始，AeX<f>oi 比 Ao\<j>oC 更為原始，正如 <£peVe9 在 Trpotfipoves 中比 <f)pove9 更為原始，而亞奧利亞方言的 eSoi/Te?（牙齒）比 oSovres（食者）更為原始。
（二）施密特的第二個觀察涉及那些在原本帶重音的 ν 之前 e 變為 o 的詞語，例如 κερκύρα 變為 κορκύρα。
（三）未重音之E字母，若其後跟隨A字母，則被同化；例如「E/caS^'/xeia」變為「A/caSi^eia」，「SeXapLVLOs」變為「SaAa/xi^aos」，「Kecra^Spa」變為「Kacra^opa」。
（iv）若 e 未受重音，則 A 同化於 e；例如 apery 變為 iperij，drep 與 arap 變為 irepos，dyxeAvo? 變為 iyx^Xvos。
（v）無重音的 a 被同化為隨後的 o；例如 αἰθέριος 和 οἰχόμενα；crabs 和 crowds；ἄχα 和 ὄμμα。

（六）A（在 />、a 之前或之後）趨同於 o，尤其是當其直接或間接地後跟 v 和 Fo 時，但以該 a 原本為提升的 e 為前提；例如，ὀρφός 來自 *Fαρφ́ης、ὦ ργα、ὀρχή、οπόρνη、ἱστορνῆμι、ὀλνημι。在所有這些情況下，我們對梵文 ri 會預期 ἀρ 和 ἀλ，正如對 Vritra，我們預期 Ἄρθος，但卻發現 Ὄρθος。直接或間接地後跟 v 或 Fo 此限制條件並非總是被遵守的，這由 ὀρχή 中 o 的保留在 ὀρχέο、ὀργή 中得到證明，由 Ὄρια（非 urdhva）以及 Ὄρθος 而非 Ἀρθος = 梵文 Vritra 也得到證明。
（vii）初始無重音的 o 與 a 同化；例如 oorrafcds、crab 及 do-ra/cd?；dcrrpdyaAos 變成 do-TpdyaXos；oSayfxos 及 dSay/xds（參見 68d£）。
